items.front: What are the three minimum required information items for inflight emergencies under 10-2-1?
items.back: 1. Aircraft identification and type. 2. Nature of the emergency. 3. Pilot's desires.

items.front: When does 10-2-1 say to start emergency assistance?
items.back: As soon as enough information has been obtained upon which to act. Do not wait for the full supplementary list.

items.front: What is the rule for requesting supplementary information from low-altitude military fighter-type aircraft under 10-2-1?
items.back: Normally do not request it. However, request the position of an aircraft that is not visually sighted or displayed on radar if the location is not given by the pilot.

items.front: When should an emergency aircraft's frequency be changed under 10-2-2?
items.back: Only when there is a valid reason. It might be best to keep the aircraft on the initial contact frequency even though 121.5 and 243.0 are emergency frequencies.

items.front: What four recognized methods may be used to orient an aircraft under 10-2-3?
items.back: Radar, NAVAIDs, pilotage, and sighting by other aircraft. Choose the means most appropriate to the circumstances.

items.front: When may ATC recommend an altitude change for an emergency aircraft under 10-2-4?
items.back: When considered necessary and weather and circumstances permit, recommend maintaining or increasing altitude to improve communications or radar. Do NOT recommend climb if the aircraft is in volcanic ash where terrain permits.

items.front: What are the eight conditions that require considering an aircraft emergency and notifying RCC/ARTCC under 10-2-5?
items.back: a. Emergency declared by pilot, facility, officials, or system. b. Unexpected loss of radar AND radio. c. Forced landing indicated. d. Crew abandon. e. Emergency code displayed. f. Intercept/escort needed. g. Ground rescue likely. h. ELT signal heard.

items.front: What is the USAF exception under 10-2-5?
items.back: USAF facilities are only required to notify the ARTCC (not the RCC).

items.front: What code is assigned when a pilot verbally notifies ATC of a hijacking under 10-2-6?
items.back: Code 7500. Phraseology: '(Identification) SQUAWK SEVEN FIVE ZERO ZERO.'

items.front: What phraseology verifies a squawked 7500 under 10-2-6?
items.back: '(Identification)(facility) VERIFY SQUAWKING SEVEN FIVE ZERO ZERO.'

items.front: What is the communication restriction after assigning or verifying code 7500 under 10-2-6?
items.back: No reference to the hijacking must be made in subsequent communications unless initiated by the pilot or directed by the DEN Air Traffic Security Coordinator. Immediately inform the operations manager, supervisor, or CIC.

items.front: What assumption is made if a pilot acknowledges code 7500 and then fails to communicate further?
items.back: Assume the flight is being subject to hijack.

items.front: What steps does 10-2-7 require for a VFR aircraft in weather difficulty needing another facility?
items.back: Determine the facility best able to provide service. If a frequency change is needed, advise the pilot of the reason, request contact with that facility, and inform the facility of the situation. If unable to communicate, relay information and clearances.

items.front: What two questions must be asked first under 10-2-8 for VFR aircraft requesting radar assistance in weather difficulty?
items.back: Is the pilot qualified for IFR flight? Is the aircraft capable of IFR flight? Both must be true to proceed with an IFR clearance.

items.front: When may radar assistance be provided to a VFR pilot who is not IFR qualified or refuses to file IFR under 10-2-8?
items.back: If the pilot declares an emergency, OR if the pilot refuses to declare one but the controller determines the exact nature of the radar services desired.

items.front: List the radar assistance techniques for non-IFR-qualified pilots under 10-2-9.
items.back: a. Avoid frequency changes except for clear channel. b. Make turns in VFR so aircraft flies straight in IFR. c. Lower gear and slow to approach speed in VFR. d. Avoid climb/descent while turning in IFR. e. Avoid abrupt maneuvers. f. Vector to VFR conditions.

items.front: What is the ELT notification path for en route vs terminal facilities under 10-2-10?
items.back: EN ROUTE: Notify RCC directly. TERMINAL: Notify ARTCC which coordinates with RCC. Terminal never contacts RCC directly for ELT events.

items.front: When is operational ground testing of ELTs authorized under 10-2-10?
items.back: During the first 5 minutes of each hour, restricted to no more than three audio sweeps.

items.front: May air traffic personnel leave their duty stations to locate an ELT signal source?
items.back: No. Air traffic personnel must NOT leave their required duty stations to locate an ELT signal source. Portable receivers may be loaned to responsible airport personnel or local authorities.

items.front: What is the first action when any bomb threat is received under 10-2-11?
items.back: Notify your supervisor or the facility air traffic manager. If general in nature, handle as 'Suspicious Activity.'

items.front: What must controllers NEVER do when a pilot needs technical assistance for a bomb threat under 10-2-11?
items.back: Do NOT suggest what actions the pilot should take concerning a bomb. Instead, obtain aircraft type/series/model, precise location/description of the device if known, and other pertinent details, then notify the supervisor who contacts the FAA aviation explosives expert.

items.front: What spacing should other aircraft maintain from a suspect aircraft on the ground under 10-2-11?
items.back: At least 100 yards if able, when it is impractical for the suspect aircraft to taxi to an isolated area or the pilot takes alternative action.

items.front: What are the three steps for handling an explosive detection K-9 team request under 10-2-12?
items.back: 1. Obtain aircraft identification and position, advise supervisor. 2. Relay nearest K-9 location to pilot when received. 3. If pilot diverts, obtain ETA and advise supervisor.

items.front: What is the landing clearance rule during a real MANPADS threat under 10-2-13?
items.back: Do NOT withhold landing clearance. The pilot or parent company determines the pilot's actions.

items.front: When may controller-to-pilot MANPADS transmissions stop under 10-2-13?
items.back: When the MANPADS information is broadcast via ATIS AND pilots indicate they have received the appropriate ATIS code. Both conditions must be met.

items.front: What additional element is included when transmitting a MANPADS alert to an individual aircraft?
items.back: SAY INTENTIONS. For all-aircraft broadcasts, this element is omitted.

items.front: What is the laser event broadcast requirement under 10-2-14?
items.back: Broadcast a general caution warning on all appropriate frequencies every 5 minutes for 20 minutes following the LAST report. Terminal facilities also include the event on ATIS for one hour following the last report.

items.front: What details must terminal ATIS include for a laser event under 10-2-14?
items.back: Time, location, altitude, color, and direction of the laser as reported by the pilot.

items.front: What are the seven factors to consider when recommending an emergency airport under 10-2-15?
items.back: 1. Remaining fuel vs airport distances. 2. Weather conditions. 3. Airport conditions. 4. NAVAID status. 5. Aircraft type. 6. Pilot's qualifications. 7. Vectoring or homing capability.

items.front: What limitation does the Emergency Autoland system have when selecting an airport?
items.back: It does NOT consider closed runways, equipment on the runway, construction, or other possible airport hazards. Controllers must supplement with their own knowledge.

items.front: What five methods may be used to guide an aircraft to an emergency airport under 10-2-16?
items.back: 1. Radar. 2. Following another aircraft. 3. NAVAIDs. 4. Pilotage by landmarks. 5. Compass headings.

items.front: When may the EOVM/EAM be used under 10-2-17?
items.back: Only when: 1. The pilot has declared an emergency, OR 2. The controller has determined an emergency exists or is imminent because the pilot cannot maintain an appropriate terrain/obstacle clearance minimum altitude.

items.front: What must the controller tell the pilot when providing EOVM/EAM emergency vectoring?
items.back: That any headings issued are emergency advisories intended only to direct the aircraft toward and over an area of lower terrain/obstacle elevation.

items.front: Do EOVM altitudes and obstructions include clearance buffers?
items.back: No. They show actual altitudes and locations of obstacle/terrain and contain NO lateral or vertical buffers for obstruction clearance.

items.front: What are the three mandatory actions when an aircraft enters volcanic ash and indicates distress under 10-2-18?
items.back: 1. Consider the aircraft in an emergency situation. 2. Do NOT initiate climb clearances to turbine-powered aircraft until the aircraft exits the ash cloud. 3. Do NOT attempt escape vectors without pilot concurrence.

items.front: What should ATC do if volcanic ash is known or forecast to be present under 10-2-18?
items.back: Relay all available information to pilots so they are aware of the ash cloud's position and altitude(s), and suggest appropriate reroutes to avoid the area.

items.front: Can airborne or ATC radar normally detect volcanic ash clouds?
items.back: No. Volcanic ash clouds are not normally detected by airborne or air traffic radar systems.

items.front: What seven items must be obtained when a death, illness, or public health risk is reported on board under 10-2-19?
items.back: 1. Call sign. 2. Number of suspected illness cases. 3. Nature of illness/risk if known. 4. Number of persons on board. 5. Number of deaths if applicable. 6. Pilot's intent. 7. Any request for assistance.

items.front: Who must relay public health risk information to the DEN under 10-2-19?
items.back: The OMIC (Operations Manager in Charge), OS (Operations Supervisor), or CIC (Controller-in-Charge) as soon as possible.

items.front: What is the DEN direct phone number under 10-2-19?
items.back: 844-432-2962 (toll free), for facilities not actively monitoring the DEN or without a dedicated line.
